You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after clicking the left vertical bar. I need to see if the vertical bar was removed too, or if something else happened.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
ascii_after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== Current rows 42-47 ===")
for i in range(42, 48):
    print(f"Row {i}: {ascii_after[i]}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print()
print("=== Gray (w) nodes ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'w':
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
